{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|鳥居}}]] [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|本殿}}]] '''三宅神社'''（みやけじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|三重県|Q128196}}{{jalink|鈴鹿市|Q207311}}国府町にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|式内社}}{{jalink|論社|Q135022904}}、{{jalink|伊勢国|Q864166}}{{jalink|総社|Q1107129}}推定社で、{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}。 {{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}末年に同じく式内社論社の'''江神社'''（えじんじゃ、旧村社）を合祀している。 === 祭神 === 現在の祭神は次の3柱[http://www.amigo.ne.jp/~chitonr2/jinjaichiran_suzuka.html 三重県の神社一覧 > 鈴鹿市]（三重県神社庁）。。 * {{jalink|国之常立神|Q478504|國常立之命}} * {{jalink|大国主|Q276944|大穴牟遅命}} * {{jalink|サルタヒコ|Q3090037|猿田彦命}} - 合祀した江神社祭神。 明治の神社明細帳では、祭神を国常立命・大穴牟遅命・{{jalink|トヨウケビメ|Q2983724|豊受比売命}}の3柱とする{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。また、明治末年の国府町各村の神社合祀により、現在は24柱を祀るともいう{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。 === 歴史 === 創建は不詳。 {{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）成立の『{{jalink|延喜式|Q1342448}}』{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932|神名帳}}では{{jalink|伊勢国|Q864166}}{{jalink|鈴鹿郡|Q278828}}に「三宅神社」の記載があり、当社はその{{jalink|式内社}}の{{jalink|論社|Q135022904}}とされている{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。他の論社としては鈴鹿市三宅町の{{jalink|三宅神社 (鈴鹿市三宅町)||三宅神社}}が挙げられている{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。「三宅」とは古代朝廷の直轄地である「{{jalink|屯倉|Q11465761}}（みやけ）」に由来すると見られ、鈴鹿市三宅町の方が鈴鹿の屯倉および式内社「三宅神社」の所在としては妥当とされる{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。当社の場合は江戸時代まで「惣社大明神」と通称され、「三宅神社」に改称したのは明治以降になり、古く「三宅神社」と称した例も知られていない{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。なおこの式内社「三宅神社」に関しては、『{{jalink|日本書紀|Q813935}}』{{jalink|天武天皇|Q335181}}元年（{{jalink|672年|Q30601}}）6月24日条で大海人皇子（天武天皇）が伊勢鈴鹿に至った時に出迎えたと見える「国司守三宅連石床」との関連性が指摘される{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}{{Sfn|三宅神社（平凡社）|1983年}}。別説として、当社を{{jalink|河曲郡|Q11553861}}の「大鹿三宅神社」に比定する説もある{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。 また当社に関しては、伊勢国の{{jalink|総社|Q1107129}}に位置づける説が知られる{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}{{Sfn|三宅神社（平凡社）|1983年}}。総社とは、各{{jalink|令制国|Q860290}}の{{jalink|国府|Q1629088}}近くに設けられた国内諸神を合祀した神社を指し、{{jalink|平安時代|Q193292}}頃から全国で出現する。伊勢国の国府の場合には、{{jalink|奈良時代|Q189178}}中頃の{{jalink|国衙|Q11421342}}跡は三宅神社から離れた{{jalink|伊勢国府跡|Q108293381|長者屋敷遺跡}}（鈴鹿市広瀬町・西富田町、国の史跡「伊勢国府跡」）と判明しているが、奈良時代後期から平安時代にかけての国衙跡の所在は明らかでなく、一説には三宅神社の鎮座する国府町付近と推定されている「伊勢国府」『日本歴史地名大系 24 三重県の地名』 平凡社、1983年。。国府町一帯では、三宅神社遺跡（境内から東南方）や天王山西遺跡などで国府関連と見られる遺構が見つかっているが、中枢となる国衙跡は発見されていない{{PDFlink|[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/kokufu-p.pdf 「国史跡伊勢国府跡 -三重県鈴鹿市長者屋敷遺跡の発掘調査-」]}}（鈴鹿市考古博物館）。[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/site100/0495.htm 三宅神社遺跡]（鈴鹿市考古博物館ホームページ）。。当社の社名「三宅」が古くからの由緒を持つ（明治期の創出ではない）ものだと仮定すれば、この「三宅」の起こりを伊勢国府の正倉に由来すると見る説もある{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。なお以上の説の一方で、{{jalink|中世|Q12554}}以来に事実上の総社として機能したと考えられる神社として{{jalink|伊奈冨神社|Q135039344}}（鈴鹿市稲生西、式内社「伊奈富神社」）がある{{Sfn|一宮制|2000年|pp=86-89}}。 {{jalink|明治維新|Q8707}}後、{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}6年（{{jalink|1873年|Q7757}}）3月に{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534}}において{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}に列した{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。江戸時代までは「惣社大明神」と通称されたが、その時に「三宅神社」と改称している{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。また、明治41年（{{jalink|1908年|Q2056}}）3月および明治44年（{{jalink|1911年|Q2076}}）5月には国府町各村の神社が合祀された{{Sfn|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}。合祀社のうちには江神社があり、こちらも鈴鹿郡の式内社「江神社」の論社であった（亀山市下ノ庄町に他論社の江神社がある）{{Sfn|三宅神社（平凡社）|1983年}}。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === 参考文献 === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=式内社研究会|author=井後政晏|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=三宅神社|year=1977|title=式内社調査報告 第7巻|publisher={{jalink|皇學館大学|Q1797584}}出版部|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|三宅神社（式内社）|1977年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=三宅神社|title={{jalink|日本歴史地名大系|Q17213509}} 24 三重県の地名|publisher={{jalink|平凡社|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|三宅神社（平凡社）|1983年}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=中世諸国一宮制研究会|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=中世諸国一宮制の基礎的研究|publisher=岩田書院|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|一宮制|2000年}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Ichiken-sha {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = Sōsha Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 10|Q2921}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Miyake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Honden}}]] '''Miyake Shrine''' (みやけじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Kō-chō, {{jalink|Suzuka|Q207311}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}. It is a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, is presumed to be the {{jalink|sōsha|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and its former rank under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. At the end of the Meiji period it merged '''E Shrine''' (えじんじゃ, former village shrine), another candidate shrine for a shikinaisha. === Enshrined deities === Current enshrined deities are the following three[http://www.amigo.ne.jp/~chitonr2/jinjaichiran_suzuka.html List of shrines in Mie Prefecture > Suzuka City] (Mie Prefecture Shrine Office).. * {{jalink|Kuninotokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} - deity of the merged E Shrine. In Meiji shrine registers, the deities are listed as Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto, Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke-hime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It is also said that after Meiji-end mergers of village shrines in Kō-chō, 24 deities are now enshrined{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. === History === The founding is unknown. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' completed in {{jalink|927|Q30470}} ({{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5), the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}'' records “Miyake Shrine” in Suzuka District, {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for that shikinaisha{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Another candidate is {{jalink|Miyake Shrine (Miyake-chō, Suzuka)||Miyake Shrine}} in Miyake-chō, Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. “Miyake” is thought to derive from ancient court-controlled lands called {{jalink|miyake (屯倉)|Q11465761}}; from that viewpoint, Miyake-chō is often considered more plausible as the location of Suzuka’s miyake and the shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine”{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. This shrine, by contrast, was commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” until the Edo period, and appears to have been renamed “Miyake Shrine” only after Meiji; no old example of this site being called “Miyake Shrine” is known{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Regarding this shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine,” a possible relation has also been pointed out to “Kokushi no kami Miyake no Muraji Iwatoko” in the ''{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}'', entry for June 24, first year of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} ({{jalink|672|Q30601}}), when Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu) reached Ise Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Another theory identifies this shrine instead with “Ōshika Miyake Shrine” in {{jalink|Kawawa District|Q11553861}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. There is also a known theory placing this shrine as the sōsha of Ise Province{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. A sōsha is a shrine near a provincial capital where provincial deities were jointly enshrined, appearing nationwide from around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. For Ise Province, the mid-{{jalink|Nara period|Q189178}} provincial office site has been identified as the Chōjayashiki ruins (national historic site “Ise Kokufu ruins”) away from Miyake Shrine, but the later Nara to Heian provincial office site remains uncertain, with one theory placing it around present Kō-chō where this shrine stands“Ise Kokufu,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 24: Place Names of Mie Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.. Around Kō-chō, remains related to the provincial capital have been found at Miyake Shrine ruins and Tennōyama-nishi ruins, but the core kokuga site has not been discovered{{PDFlink|[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/kokufu-p.pdf "National Historic Site Ise Kokufu Ruins - Excavation of Chōjayashiki Site, Suzuka, Mie"]}} (Suzuka Archaeological Museum).[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/site100/0495.htm Miyake Shrine Ruins] (Suzuka Archaeological Museum website).. If the shrine name “Miyake” is assumed to be old in origin (not created in Meiji), another theory derives this “Miyake” from the Ise provincial capital granary{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in March {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6), it was ranked as a {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It had been commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” through the Edo period, and was renamed “Miyake Shrine” then{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In March {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} and May {{jalink|1911|Q2076}}, shrines from villages in Kō-chō were merged{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Among merged shrines was E Shrine, itself a candidate for the Suzuka District shikinaisha “E Shrine” (another candidate exists in Shimo-no-shō-chō, Kameyama){{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society|author=Igo Masaaki|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=Miyake Shrine|year=1977|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha Vol. 7|publisher={{jalink|Kogakkan University|Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Miyake Shrine|title={{jalink|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509}} 24 Place Names of Mie Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Study on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya system|2000}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:総社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社0]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}

It had been commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” through the Edo period, and was renamed “Miyake Shrine” then{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In March {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} and May {{jalink|1911|Q2076}}, shrines from villages in Kō-chō were merged{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Among merged shrines was E Shrine, itself a candidate for the Suzuka District shikinaisha “E Shrine” (another candidate exists in Shimo-no-shō-chō, Kameyama){{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society|author=Igo Masaaki|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=Miyake Shrine|year=1977|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha Vol. 7|publisher={{jalink|Kogakkan University|Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Miyake Shrine|title={{jalink|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509}} 24 Place Names of Mie Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Study on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya system|2000}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:総社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社0]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: used local AI agents to translate these pages. Contrary to the description it was actually Claude Code for a few, OpenClaw with Codex, and regular Codex. Ollama and DeepSeek were too bad to actually use. see categorization since some of them are very questionable, especially if there was a massive decrease in page size or the {{jawiki| template is minimal or absent on the page

Miyake Shrine |画像 = [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) haiden.JPG|280px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Suzuka|Q207311}} Kō-chō 1609 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Kuninotokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）{{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}}
(presumed) {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}} {{jalink|sōsha|Q1107129}}
former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Ichiken-sha {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = Sōsha Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 10|Q2921}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Miyake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Honden}}]] '''Miyake Shrine''' (みやけじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Kō-chō, {{jalink|Suzuka|Q207311}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}. It is a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, is presumed to be the {{jalink|sōsha|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and its former rank under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. At the end of the Meiji period it merged '''E Shrine''' (えじんじゃ, former village shrine), another candidate shrine for a shikinaisha. === Enshrined deities === Current enshrined deities are the following three[http://www.amigo.ne.jp/~chitonr2/jinjaichiran_suzuka.html List of shrines in Mie Prefecture > Suzuka City] (Mie Prefecture Shrine Office).. * {{jalink|Kuninotokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} - deity of the merged E Shrine. In Meiji shrine registers, the deities are listed as Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto, Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke-hime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It is also said that after Meiji-end mergers of village shrines in Kō-chō, 24 deities are now enshrined{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. === History === The founding is unknown. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' completed in {{jalink|927|Q30470}} ({{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5), the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}'' records “Miyake Shrine” in Suzuka District, {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for that shikinaisha{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Another candidate is {{jalink|Miyake Shrine (Miyake-chō, Suzuka)||Miyake Shrine}} in Miyake-chō, Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. “Miyake” is thought to derive from ancient court-controlled lands called {{jalink|miyake (屯倉)|Q11465761}}; from that viewpoint, Miyake-chō is often considered more plausible as the location of Suzuka’s miyake and the shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine”{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. This shrine, by contrast, was commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” until the Edo period, and appears to have been renamed “Miyake Shrine” only after Meiji; no old example of this site being called “Miyake Shrine” is known{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Regarding this shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine,” a possible relation has also been pointed out to “Kokushi no kami Miyake no Muraji Iwatoko” in the ''{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}'', entry for June 24, first year of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} ({{jalink|672|Q30601}}), when Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu) reached Ise Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Another theory identifies this shrine instead with “Ōshika Miyake Shrine” in {{jalink|Kawawa District|Q11553861}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. There is also a known theory placing this shrine as the sōsha of Ise Province{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. A sōsha is a shrine near a provincial capital where provincial deities were jointly enshrined, appearing nationwide from around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. For Ise Province, the mid-{{jalink|Nara period|Q189178}} provincial office site has been identified as the Chōjayashiki ruins (national historic site “Ise Kokufu ruins”) away from Miyake Shrine, but the later Nara to Heian provincial office site remains uncertain, with one theory placing it around present Kō-chō where this shrine stands“Ise Kokufu,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 24: Place Names of Mie Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.. Around Kō-chō, remains related to the provincial capital have been found at Miyake Shrine ruins and Tennōyama-nishi ruins, but the core kokuga site has not been discovered{{PDFlink|[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/kokufu-p.pdf "National Historic Site Ise Kokufu Ruins - Excavation of Chōjayashiki Site, Suzuka, Mie"]}} (Suzuka Archaeological Museum).[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/site100/0495.htm Miyake Shrine Ruins] (Suzuka Archaeological Museum website).. If the shrine name “Miyake” is assumed to be old in origin (not created in Meiji), another theory derives this “Miyake” from the Ise provincial capital granary{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in March {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6), it was ranked as a {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It had been commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” through the Edo period, and was renamed “Miyake Shrine” then{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In March {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} and May {{jalink|1911|Q2076}}, shrines from villages in Kō-chō were merged{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Among merged shrines was E Shrine, itself a candidate for the Suzuka District shikinaisha “E Shrine” (another candidate exists in Shimo-no-shō-chō, Kameyama){{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society|author=Igo Masaaki|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=Miyake Shrine|year=1977|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha Vol. 7|publisher={{jalink|Kogakkan University|Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Miyake Shrine|title={{jalink|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509}} 24 Place Names of Mie Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Study on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya system|2000}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:総社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社0]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}

(presumed) {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}} {{jalink|sōsha|Q1107129}}
former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Ichiken-sha {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = Sōsha Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 10|Q2921}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Miyake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Honden}}]] '''Miyake Shrine''' (みやけじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Kō-chō, {{jalink|Suzuka|Q207311}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}. It is a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, is presumed to be the {{jalink|sōsha|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and its former rank under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. At the end of the Meiji period it merged '''E Shrine''' (えじんじゃ, former village shrine), another candidate shrine for a shikinaisha. === Enshrined deities === Current enshrined deities are the following three[http://www.amigo.ne.jp/~chitonr2/jinjaichiran_suzuka.html List of shrines in Mie Prefecture > Suzuka City] (Mie Prefecture Shrine Office).. * {{jalink|Kuninotokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} - deity of the merged E Shrine. In Meiji shrine registers, the deities are listed as Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto, Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke-hime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It is also said that after Meiji-end mergers of village shrines in Kō-chō, 24 deities are now enshrined{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. === History === The founding is unknown. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' completed in {{jalink|927|Q30470}} ({{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5), the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}'' records “Miyake Shrine” in Suzuka District, {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for that shikinaisha{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Another candidate is {{jalink|Miyake Shrine (Miyake-chō, Suzuka)||Miyake Shrine}} in Miyake-chō, Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. “Miyake” is thought to derive from ancient court-controlled lands called {{jalink|miyake (屯倉)|Q11465761}}; from that viewpoint, Miyake-chō is often considered more plausible as the location of Suzuka’s miyake and the shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine”{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. This shrine, by contrast, was commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” until the Edo period, and appears to have been renamed “Miyake Shrine” only after Meiji; no old example of this site being called “Miyake Shrine” is known{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Regarding this shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine,” a possible relation has also been pointed out to “Kokushi no kami Miyake no Muraji Iwatoko” in the ''{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}'', entry for June 24, first year of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} ({{jalink|672|Q30601}}), when Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu) reached Ise Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Another theory identifies this shrine instead with “Ōshika Miyake Shrine” in {{jalink|Kawawa District|Q11553861}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. There is also a known theory placing this shrine as the sōsha of Ise Province{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. A sōsha is a shrine near a provincial capital where provincial deities were jointly enshrined, appearing nationwide from around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. For Ise Province, the mid-{{jalink|Nara period|Q189178}} provincial office site has been identified as the Chōjayashiki ruins (national historic site “Ise Kokufu ruins”) away from Miyake Shrine, but the later Nara to Heian provincial office site remains uncertain, with one theory placing it around present Kō-chō where this shrine stands“Ise Kokufu,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 24: Place Names of Mie Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.. Around Kō-chō, remains related to the provincial capital have been found at Miyake Shrine ruins and Tennōyama-nishi ruins, but the core kokuga site has not been discovered{{PDFlink|[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/kokufu-p.pdf "National Historic Site Ise Kokufu Ruins - Excavation of Chōjayashiki Site, Suzuka, Mie"]}} (Suzuka Archaeological Museum).[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/site100/0495.htm Miyake Shrine Ruins] (Suzuka Archaeological Museum website).. If the shrine name “Miyake” is assumed to be old in origin (not created in Meiji), another theory derives this “Miyake” from the Ise provincial capital granary{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in March {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6), it was ranked as a {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It had been commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” through the Edo period, and was renamed “Miyake Shrine” then{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In March {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} and May {{jalink|1911|Q2076}}, shrines from villages in Kō-chō were merged{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Among merged shrines was E Shrine, itself a candidate for the Suzuka District shikinaisha “E Shrine” (another candidate exists in Shimo-no-shō-chō, Kameyama){{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society|author=Igo Masaaki|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=Miyake Shrine|year=1977|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha Vol. 7|publisher={{jalink|Kogakkan University|Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Miyake Shrine|title={{jalink|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509}} 24 Place Names of Mie Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Study on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya system|2000}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:総社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社0]] [[Category:Need translation]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}

In Meiji shrine registers, the deities are listed as Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto, Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke-hime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It is also said that after Meiji-end mergers of village shrines in Kō-chō, 24 deities are now enshrined{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. === History === The founding is unknown. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' completed in {{jalink|927|Q30470}} ({{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5), the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}'' records “Miyake Shrine” in Suzuka District, {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for that shikinaisha{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Another candidate is {{jalink|Miyake Shrine (Miyake-chō, Suzuka)||Miyake Shrine}} in Miyake-chō, Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. “Miyake” is thought to derive from ancient court-controlled lands called {{jalink|miyake (屯倉)|Q11465761}}; from that viewpoint, Miyake-chō is often considered more plausible as the location of Suzuka’s miyake and the shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine”{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. This shrine, by contrast, was commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” until the Edo period, and appears to have been renamed “Miyake Shrine” only after Meiji; no old example of this site being called “Miyake Shrine” is known{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Regarding this shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine,” a possible relation has also been pointed out to “Kokushi no kami Miyake no Muraji Iwatoko” in the ''{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}'', entry for June 24, first year of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} ({{jalink|672|Q30601}}), when Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu) reached Ise Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Another theory identifies this shrine instead with “Ōshika Miyake Shrine” in {{jalink|Kawawa District|Q11553861}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. There is also a known theory placing this shrine as the sōsha of Ise Province{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. A sōsha is a shrine near a provincial capital where provincial deities were jointly enshrined, appearing nationwide from around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. For Ise Province, the mid-{{jalink|Nara period|Q189178}} provincial office site has been identified as the Chōjayashiki ruins (national historic site “Ise Kokufu ruins”) away from Miyake Shrine, but the later Nara to Heian provincial office site remains uncertain, with one theory placing it around present Kō-chō where this shrine stands“Ise Kokufu,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 24: Place Names of Mie Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.. Around Kō-chō, remains related to the provincial capital have been found at Miyake Shrine ruins and Tennōyama-nishi ruins, but the core kokuga site has not been discovered{{PDFlink|[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/kokufu-p.pdf "National Historic Site Ise Kokufu Ruins - Excavation of Chōjayashiki Site, Suzuka, Mie"]}} (Suzuka Archaeological Museum).[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/site100/0495.htm Miyake Shrine Ruins] (Suzuka Archaeological Museum website).. If the shrine name “Miyake” is assumed to be old in origin (not created in Meiji), another theory derives this “Miyake” from the Ise provincial capital granary{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in March {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6), it was ranked as a {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It had been commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” through the Edo period, and was renamed “Miyake Shrine” then{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In March {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} and May {{jalink|1911|Q2076}}, shrines from villages in Kō-chō were merged{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Among merged shrines was E Shrine, itself a candidate for the Suzuka District shikinaisha “E Shrine” (another candidate exists in Shimo-no-shō-chō, Kameyama){{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society|author=Igo Masaaki|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=Miyake Shrine|year=1977|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha Vol. 7|publisher={{jalink|Kogakkan University|Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Miyake Shrine|title={{jalink|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509}} 24 Place Names of Mie Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Study on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya system|2000}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:総社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社0]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}

(presumed) {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}} {{jalink|sōsha|Q1107129}}
former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Ichiken-sha {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = Sōsha Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 10|Q2921}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Miyake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Honden}}]] '''Miyake Shrine''' (みやけじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Kō-chō, {{jalink|Suzuka|Q207311}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}. It is a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, is presumed to be the {{jalink|sōsha|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and its former rank under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. At the end of the Meiji period it merged '''E Shrine''' (えじんじゃ, former village shrine), another candidate shrine for a shikinaisha. === Enshrined deities === Current enshrined deities are the following three[http://www.amigo.ne.jp/~chitonr2/jinjaichiran_suzuka.html List of shrines in Mie Prefecture > Suzuka City] (Mie Prefecture Shrine Office).. * {{jalink|Kuninotokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} - deity of the merged E Shrine. In Meiji shrine registers, the deities are listed as Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto, Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke-hime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It is also said that after Meiji-end mergers of village shrines in Kō-chō, 24 deities are now enshrined{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. === History === The founding is unknown. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' completed in {{jalink|927|Q30470}} ({{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5), the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}'' records “Miyake Shrine” in Suzuka District, {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for that shikinaisha{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Another candidate is {{jalink|Miyake Shrine (Miyake-chō, Suzuka)||Miyake Shrine}} in Miyake-chō, Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. “Miyake” is thought to derive from ancient court-controlled lands called {{jalink|miyake (屯倉)|Q11465761}}; from that viewpoint, Miyake-chō is often considered more plausible as the location of Suzuka’s miyake and the shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine”{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. This shrine, by contrast, was commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” until the Edo period, and appears to have been renamed “Miyake Shrine” only after Meiji; no old example of this site being called “Miyake Shrine” is known{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Regarding this shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine,” a possible relation has also been pointed out to “Kokushi no kami Miyake no Muraji Iwatoko” in the ''{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}'', entry for June 24, first year of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} ({{jalink|672|Q30601}}), when Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu) reached Ise Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Another theory identifies this shrine instead with “Ōshika Miyake Shrine” in {{jalink|Kawawa District|Q11553861}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. There is also a known theory placing this shrine as the sōsha of Ise Province{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. A sōsha is a shrine near a provincial capital where provincial deities were jointly enshrined, appearing nationwide from around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. For Ise Province, the mid-{{jalink|Nara period|Q189178}} provincial office site has been identified as the Chōjayashiki ruins (national historic site “Ise Kokufu ruins”) away from Miyake Shrine, but the later Nara to Heian provincial office site remains uncertain, with one theory placing it around present Kō-chō where this shrine stands“Ise Kokufu,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 24: Place Names of Mie Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.. Around Kō-chō, remains related to the provincial capital have been found at Miyake Shrine ruins and Tennōyama-nishi ruins, but the core kokuga site has not been discovered{{PDFlink|[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/kokufu-p.pdf "National Historic Site Ise Kokufu Ruins - Excavation of Chōjayashiki Site, Suzuka, Mie"]}} (Suzuka Archaeological Museum).[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/site100/0495.htm Miyake Shrine Ruins] (Suzuka Archaeological Museum website).. If the shrine name “Miyake” is assumed to be old in origin (not created in Meiji), another theory derives this “Miyake” from the Ise provincial capital granary{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in March {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6), it was ranked as a {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It had been commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” through the Edo period, and was renamed “Miyake Shrine” then{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In March {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} and May {{jalink|1911|Q2076}}, shrines from villages in Kō-chō were merged{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Among merged shrines was E Shrine, itself a candidate for the Suzuka District shikinaisha “E Shrine” (another candidate exists in Shimo-no-shō-chō, Kameyama){{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society|author=Igo Masaaki|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=Miyake Shrine|year=1977|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha Vol. 7|publisher={{jalink|Kogakkan University|Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Miyake Shrine|title={{jalink|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509}} 24 Place Names of Mie Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Study on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya system|2000}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:総社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社0]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}[[Category:qq]]

Miyake Shrine |画像 = [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) haiden.JPG|280px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}{{jalink|Suzuka|Q207311}} Kō-chō 1609 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Kuninotokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto}}
{{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} |神体 = |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}（small）{{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}}
(presumed) {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}} {{jalink|sōsha|Q1107129}}
former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Ichiken-sha {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = Sōsha Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 10|Q2921}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Miyake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Honden}}]] '''Miyake Shrine''' (みやけじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Kō-chō, {{jalink|Suzuka|Q207311}}, {{jalink|Mie Prefecture|Q128196}}. It is a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}, is presumed to be the {{jalink|sōsha|Q1107129}} of {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and its former rank under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. At the end of the Meiji period it merged '''E Shrine''' (えじんじゃ, former village shrine), another candidate shrine for a shikinaisha. === Enshrined deities === Current enshrined deities are the following three[http://www.amigo.ne.jp/~chitonr2/jinjaichiran_suzuka.html List of shrines in Mie Prefecture > Suzuka City] (Mie Prefecture Shrine Office).. * {{jalink|Kuninotokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Ōkuninushi|Q276944|Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} - deity of the merged E Shrine. In Meiji shrine registers, the deities are listed as Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto, Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{jalink|Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|Toyouke-hime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It is also said that after Meiji-end mergers of village shrines in Kō-chō, 24 deities are now enshrined{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. === History === The founding is unknown. In the ''{{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}}'' completed in {{jalink|927|Q30470}} ({{jalink|Enchō|Q1327274}} 5), the ''{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|Jinmyōchō}}'' records “Miyake Shrine” in Suzuka District, {{jalink|Ise Province|Q864166}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{jalink|candidate shrine|Q135022904}} for that shikinaisha{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Another candidate is {{jalink|Miyake Shrine (Miyake-chō, Suzuka)||Miyake Shrine}} in Miyake-chō, Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. “Miyake” is thought to derive from ancient court-controlled lands called {{jalink|miyake (屯倉)|Q11465761}}; from that viewpoint, Miyake-chō is often considered more plausible as the location of Suzuka’s miyake and the shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine”{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. This shrine, by contrast, was commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” until the Edo period, and appears to have been renamed “Miyake Shrine” only after Meiji; no old example of this site being called “Miyake Shrine” is known{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Regarding this shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine,” a possible relation has also been pointed out to “Kokushi no kami Miyake no Muraji Iwatoko” in the ''{{jalink|Nihon Shoki|Q813935}}'', entry for June 24, first year of {{jalink|Emperor Tenmu|Q335181}} ({{jalink|672|Q30601}}), when Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu) reached Ise Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Another theory identifies this shrine instead with “Ōshika Miyake Shrine” in {{jalink|Kawawa District|Q11553861}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. There is also a known theory placing this shrine as the sōsha of Ise Province{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. A sōsha is a shrine near a provincial capital where provincial deities were jointly enshrined, appearing nationwide from around the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. For Ise Province, the mid-{{jalink|Nara period|Q189178}} provincial office site has been identified as the Chōjayashiki ruins (national historic site “Ise Kokufu ruins”) away from Miyake Shrine, but the later Nara to Heian provincial office site remains uncertain, with one theory placing it around present Kō-chō where this shrine stands“Ise Kokufu,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 24: Place Names of Mie Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.. Around Kō-chō, remains related to the provincial capital have been found at Miyake Shrine ruins and Tennōyama-nishi ruins, but the core kokuga site has not been discovered{{PDFlink|[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/kokufu-p.pdf "National Historic Site Ise Kokufu Ruins - Excavation of Chōjayashiki Site, Suzuka, Mie"]}} (Suzuka Archaeological Museum).[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/site100/0495.htm Miyake Shrine Ruins] (Suzuka Archaeological Museum website).. If the shrine name “Miyake” is assumed to be old in origin (not created in Meiji), another theory derives this “Miyake” from the Ise provincial capital granary{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. After the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}, in March {{jalink|1873|Q7757}} (Meiji 6), it was ranked as a {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the {{jalink|Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It had been commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” through the Edo period, and was renamed “Miyake Shrine” then{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In March {{jalink|1908|Q2056}} and May {{jalink|1911|Q2076}}, shrines from villages in Kō-chō were merged{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Among merged shrines was E Shrine, itself a candidate for the Suzuka District shikinaisha “E Shrine” (another candidate exists in Shimo-no-shō-chō, Kameyama){{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society|author=Igo Masaaki|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=Miyake Shrine|year=1977|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha Vol. 7|publisher={{jalink|Kogakkan University|Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Miyake Shrine|title={{jalink|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509}} 24 Place Names of Mie Prefecture|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Study on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya system|2000}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:総社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社0]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

'language': 'ja'} * {'text': '三重県鈴鹿市神戸石橋町', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Miyake-jinja (Suzuka)}} [[JA:三宅神社 (鈴鹿市国府町)]] {{wikidata link|Q22120521}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Miyake Shrine |画像 = [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) haiden.JPG|280px]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196}}{{ill|lt=Suzuka|Q207311|WD=Q207311}} Kō-chō 1609 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Kuninotokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|WD=Q478504|lt=Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto}}

(presumed) {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166}} {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}}
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Ichiken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = Sōsha Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 10|Q2921|WD=Q2921}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Miyake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Honden}}]] '''Miyake Shrine''' (みやけじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Kō-chō, {{ill|lt=Suzuka|Q207311|WD=Q207311}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196}}. It is a {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} for a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}, is presumed to be the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. At the end of the Meiji period it merged '''E Shrine''' (えじんじゃ, former village shrine), another candidate shrine for a shikinaisha. === Enshrined deities === Current enshrined deities are the following three[http://www.amigo.ne.jp/~chitonr2/jinjaichiran_suzuka.html List of shrines in Mie Prefecture > Suzuka City] (Mie Prefecture Shrine Office).. * {{ill|lt=Kuninotokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|WD=Q478504|lt=Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} - deity of the merged E Shrine. In Meiji shrine registers, the deities are listed as Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto, Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-hime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It is also said that after Meiji-end mergers of village shrines in Kō-chō, 24 deities are now enshrined{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. === History === The founding is unknown. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' completed in {{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}} ({{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5), the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō}}'' records “Miyake Shrine” in Suzuka District, {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} for that shikinaisha{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Another candidate is {{ill|lt=Miyake Shrine (Miyake-chō, Suzuka)||WD=|lt=Miyake Shrine}} in Miyake-chō, Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. “Miyake” is thought to derive from ancient court-controlled lands called {{ill|lt=miyake (屯倉)|Q11465761|WD=Q11465761}}; from that viewpoint, Miyake-chō is often considered more plausible as the location of Suzuka’s miyake and the shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine”{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. This shrine, by contrast, was commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” until the Edo period, and appears to have been renamed “Miyake Shrine” only after Meiji; no old example of this site being called “Miyake Shrine” is known{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Regarding this shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine,” a possible relation has also been pointed out to “Kokushi no kami Miyake no Muraji Iwatoko” in the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}'', entry for June 24, first year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181}} ({{ill|lt=672|Q30601|WD=Q30601}}), when Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu) reached Ise Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Another theory identifies this shrine instead with “Ōshika Miyake Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Kawawa District|Q11553861|WD=Q11553861}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. There is also a known theory placing this shrine as the sōsha of Ise Province{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. A sōsha is a shrine near a provincial capital where provincial deities were jointly enshrined, appearing nationwide from around the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}. For Ise Province, the mid-{{ill|lt=Nara period|Q189178|WD=Q189178}} provincial office site has been identified as the Chōjayashiki ruins (national historic site “Ise Kokufu ruins”) away from Miyake Shrine, but the later Nara to Heian provincial office site remains uncertain, with one theory placing it around present Kō-chō where this shrine stands“Ise Kokufu,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 24: Place Names of Mie Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.. Around Kō-chō, remains related to the provincial capital have been found at Miyake Shrine ruins and Tennōyama-nishi ruins, but the core kokuga site has not been discovered{{PDFlink|[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/kokufu-p.pdf "National Historic Site Ise Kokufu Ruins - Excavation of Chōjayashiki Site, Suzuka, Mie"]}} (Suzuka Archaeological Museum).[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/site100/0495.htm Miyake Shrine Ruins] (Suzuka Archaeological Museum website).. If the shrine name “Miyake” is assumed to be old in origin (not created in Meiji), another theory derives this “Miyake” from the Ise provincial capital granary{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in March {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 6), it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It had been commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” through the Edo period, and was renamed “Miyake Shrine” then{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In March {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} and May {{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|WD=Q2076}}, shrines from villages in Kō-chō were merged{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Among merged shrines was E Shrine, itself a candidate for the Suzuka District shikinaisha “E Shrine” (another candidate exists in Shimo-no-shō-chō, Kameyama){{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society|author=Igo Masaaki|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=Miyake Shrine|year=1977|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha Vol. 7|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Miyake Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}} 24 Place Names of Mie Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Study on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya system|2000}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:総社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社0]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

This shrine, by contrast, was commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” until the Edo period, and appears to have been renamed “Miyake Shrine” only after Meiji; no old example of this site being called “Miyake Shrine” is known{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Regarding this shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine,” a possible relation has also been pointed out to “Kokushi no kami Miyake no Muraji Iwatoko” in the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}'', entry for June 24, first year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181}} ({{ill|lt=672|Q30601|WD=Q30601}}), when Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu) reached Ise Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Another theory identifies this shrine instead with “Ōshika Miyake Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Kawawa District|Q11553861|WD=Q11553861}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. There is also a known theory placing this shrine as the sōsha of Ise Province{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. A sōsha is a shrine near a provincial capital where provincial deities were jointly enshrined, appearing nationwide from around the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}. For Ise Province, the mid-{{ill|lt=Nara period|Q189178|WD=Q189178}} provincial office site has been identified as the Chōjayashiki ruins (national historic site “Ise Kokufu ruins”) away from Miyake Shrine, but the later Nara to Heian provincial office site remains uncertain, with one theory placing it around present Kō-chō where this shrine stands“Ise Kokufu,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 24: Place Names of Mie Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.. Around Kō-chō, remains related to the provincial capital have been found at Miyake Shrine ruins and Tennōyama-nishi ruins, but the core kokuga site has not been discovered{{PDFlink|[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/kokufu-p.pdf "National Historic Site Ise Kokufu Ruins - Excavation of Chōjayashiki Site, Suzuka, Mie"]}} (Suzuka Archaeological Museum).[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/site100/0495.htm Miyake Shrine Ruins] (Suzuka Archaeological Museum website).. If the shrine name “Miyake” is assumed to be old in origin (not created in Meiji), another theory derives this “Miyake” from the Ise provincial capital granary{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in March {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 6), it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It had been commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” through the Edo period, and was renamed “Miyake Shrine” then{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In March {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} and May {{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|WD=Q2076}}, shrines from villages in Kō-chō were merged{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Among merged shrines was E Shrine, itself a candidate for the Suzuka District shikinaisha “E Shrine” (another candidate exists in Shimo-no-shō-chō, Kameyama){{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society|author=Igo Masaaki|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=Miyake Shrine|year=1977|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha Vol. 7|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Miyake Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}} 24 Place Names of Mie Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Study on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya system|2000}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:総社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社0]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

(presumed) {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166}} {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}}
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = Ichiken-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = Sōsha Daimyōjin |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 10|Q2921|WD=Q2921}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Mie|label=Miyake Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) torii.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] [[File:Miyake-jinja (Kou-cho, Suzuka) honden.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Honden}}]] '''Miyake Shrine''' (みやけじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Kō-chō, {{ill|lt=Suzuka|Q207311|WD=Q207311}}, {{ill|lt=Mie Prefecture|Q128196|WD=Q128196}}. It is a {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} for a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}}, is presumed to be the {{ill|lt=sōsha|Q1107129|WD=Q1107129}} of {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. At the end of the Meiji period it merged '''E Shrine''' (えじんじゃ, former village shrine), another candidate shrine for a shikinaisha. === Enshrined deities === Current enshrined deities are the following three[http://www.amigo.ne.jp/~chitonr2/jinjaichiran_suzuka.html List of shrines in Mie Prefecture > Suzuka City] (Mie Prefecture Shrine Office).. * {{ill|lt=Kuninotokotachi-no-Kami|Q478504|WD=Q478504|lt=Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Ōkuninushi|Q276944|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko-no-Mikoto}} - deity of the merged E Shrine. In Meiji shrine registers, the deities are listed as Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto, Ōanamuchi-no-Mikoto, and {{ill|lt=Toyouke-hime|Q2983724|WD=Q2983724|lt=Toyouke-hime-no-Mikoto}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It is also said that after Meiji-end mergers of village shrines in Kō-chō, 24 deities are now enshrined{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. === History === The founding is unknown. In the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}}'' completed in {{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470}} ({{ill|lt=Enchō|Q1327274|WD=Q1327274}} 5), the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Jinmyōchō}}'' records “Miyake Shrine” in Suzuka District, {{ill|lt=Ise Province|Q864166|WD=Q864166}}, and this shrine is regarded as a {{ill|lt=candidate shrine|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904}} for that shikinaisha{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Another candidate is {{ill|lt=Miyake Shrine (Miyake-chō, Suzuka)||WD=|lt=Miyake Shrine}} in Miyake-chō, Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. “Miyake” is thought to derive from ancient court-controlled lands called {{ill|lt=miyake (屯倉)|Q11465761|WD=Q11465761}}; from that viewpoint, Miyake-chō is often considered more plausible as the location of Suzuka’s miyake and the shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine”{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. This shrine, by contrast, was commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” until the Edo period, and appears to have been renamed “Miyake Shrine” only after Meiji; no old example of this site being called “Miyake Shrine” is known{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Regarding this shikinaisha “Miyake Shrine,” a possible relation has also been pointed out to “Kokushi no kami Miyake no Muraji Iwatoko” in the ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Shoki|Q813935|WD=Q813935}}'', entry for June 24, first year of {{ill|lt=Emperor Tenmu|Q335181|WD=Q335181}} ({{ill|lt=672|Q30601|WD=Q30601}}), when Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu) reached Ise Suzuka{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. Another theory identifies this shrine instead with “Ōshika Miyake Shrine” in {{ill|lt=Kawawa District|Q11553861|WD=Q11553861}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. There is also a known theory placing this shrine as the sōsha of Ise Province{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. A sōsha is a shrine near a provincial capital where provincial deities were jointly enshrined, appearing nationwide from around the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}}. For Ise Province, the mid-{{ill|lt=Nara period|Q189178|WD=Q189178}} provincial office site has been identified as the Chōjayashiki ruins (national historic site “Ise Kokufu ruins”) away from Miyake Shrine, but the later Nara to Heian provincial office site remains uncertain, with one theory placing it around present Kō-chō where this shrine stands“Ise Kokufu,” ''Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 24: Place Names of Mie Prefecture'', Heibonsha, 1983.. Around Kō-chō, remains related to the provincial capital have been found at Miyake Shrine ruins and Tennōyama-nishi ruins, but the core kokuga site has not been discovered{{PDFlink|[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/kokufu-p.pdf "National Historic Site Ise Kokufu Ruins - Excavation of Chōjayashiki Site, Suzuka, Mie"]}} (Suzuka Archaeological Museum).[http://www.edu.city.suzuka.mie.jp/museum/site100/0495.htm Miyake Shrine Ruins] (Suzuka Archaeological Museum website).. If the shrine name “Miyake” is assumed to be old in origin (not created in Meiji), another theory derives this “Miyake” from the Ise provincial capital granary{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. After the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}, in March {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}} (Meiji 6), it was ranked as a {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534}}{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It had been commonly called “Sōsha Daimyōjin” through the Edo period, and was renamed “Miyake Shrine” then{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In March {{ill|lt=1908|Q2056|WD=Q2056}} and May {{ill|lt=1911|Q2076|WD=Q2076}}, shrines from villages in Kō-chō were merged{{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Among merged shrines was E Shrine, itself a candidate for the Suzuka District shikinaisha “E Shrine” (another candidate exists in Shimo-no-shō-chō, Kameyama){{Sfn|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Shikinaisha Research Society|author=Igo Masaaki|authorlink=井後政晏|chapter=Miyake Shrine|year=1977|title=Survey Report on Shikinaisha Vol. 7|publisher={{ill|lt=Kogakkan University|Q1797584|WD=Q1797584}} Press|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Shikinaisha)|1977}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1983|chapter=Miyake Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei|Q17213509|WD=Q17213509}} 24 Place Names of Mie Prefecture|publisher={{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}|isbn=978-4582490244|ref={{Harvid|Miyake Shrine (Heibonsha)|1983}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System Study Group|author=|year=2000|chapter=|title=Basic Study on the Medieval Provincial Ichinomiya System|publisher=Iwata Shoin|isbn=978-4872941708|ref={{Harvid|Ichinomiya system|2000}}}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやけしんしや すすかしこうちよう}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Ise Province]] [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の建築物]] [[Category:鈴鹿市の歴史]] [[Category:伊勢国の式内社]] [[Category:総社]] [[Category:三重県の旧村社]] [[Category:伊勢国|社0]] [[Category:Qsort]]{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:QMiyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}{{DEFAULTSORT:出羽神社Miyake Shrine (Q22120521)}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]
